sources.notes: Read the two-line geometry, deletion rules, all-k output requirement, and constraints: sums of n and m are at most 200000.
Official editorial and embedded accepted C++ derive two decreasing marginal-gain prefix sums, the feasible split interval, and ternary search for every k.
Consulted a full accepted O(n log n) reverse-greedy implementation that maintains the optimal count split while k decreases.
Independent round write-up confirms the extreme pairing reduction and exchange argument: aside from the unrestricted greedy split, an optimum must lie on a resource boundary.

annotation.summary: Points lie on y=0 and y=2; each operation removes a non-collinear triple and earns its triangle area. Compute the maximum operation count and the best score for every exact count k.
annotation.constraints: Up to 30000 tests; 1 <= n,m <= 200000, coordinate magnitudes are at most 1e9, and the separate sums of n and m are at most 200000. Scores require 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: A valid triangle uses two points from one line and one from the other, and because the height is 2 its area equals the horizontal distance of the same-line pair. After sorting, the best p pairs on a line are the p outermost pairs, whose marginal gains are strictly decreasing; build their prefix sums A and B. For k operations and p bases chosen on y=0, feasibility is max(0,2k-m) <= p <= min(k,n-k), and the score is A[p]+B[k-p]. Its discrete derivative is decreasing, so this function is concave/unimodal and can be maximized by integer ternary search. Feasibility also gives k_max=min(n,m,floor((n+m)/3)).
annotation.complexity: O((n+m) log(n+m) + k_max log(min(n,m)+1)) time and O(n+m) memory per test case.
tricks: Reduce area to a one-dimensional base length because the two horizontal lines are distance 2 apart.
Pair smallest with largest, second-smallest with second-largest, and so on to obtain decreasing marginal gains.
Translate point consumption into one feasible interval for the number of bases taken from the first line.
Use discrete concavity of the two prefix sums rather than enumerating every split.

solution_variants.name: concave-split-ternary-search
solution_variants.summary: Build the two extreme-pair gain prefix sums and, for every k, ternary-search A[p]+B[k-p] on its feasible integer interval, then check the final constant-size range.
solution_variants.complexity: O((n+m) log(n+m) + k_max log(min(n,m)+1)) time and O(n+m) memory

solution_variants.name: reverse-k-greedy-maintenance
solution_variants.summary: Construct the optimal capped merge of the two decreasing gain sequences at k_max. As k decreases, both per-line caps relax by one while one selected gain must be removed; drop the smaller selected tail and perform boundary swaps when a newly allowed gain beats the opposite selected tail.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n + m log m + k_max) time and O(n+m) memory

tags.evidence: Sorting each line is what exposes the optimal outermost pair sequence and its strictly decreasing marginal distances.
Every candidate split must be evaluated in O(1) as A[p]+B[k-p] during the per-k optimization.
The official main variant maximizes a discrete concave function of p on the feasible interval for each k.
The geometric area formula turns every legal triangle into the horizontal distance between its two same-line vertices.
The marginal gain sequences are generated by moving inward from the two sorted endpoints and pairing opposite extremes.
The resource inequalities first yield the exact bound k_max=min(n,m,floor((n+m)/3)) and then bound every feasible split.
The distinct reverse-k variant greedily merges decreasing gains under caps and repairs the optimum by boundary exchanges as caps relax.
The algorithm iterates over k because every f(k) must be output; it does not brute-force triangle choices or all feasible pair-count splits.
